sn2.9:1.1: At Sāvatthī. Now on that occasion the young deva Candima had been seized by Rahu, lord of the asuras.[n.156] Candima is a deva dwelling in the mansion of the moon; the word itself usually simply means the moon. Obviously his seizure by Rahu represents the lunar eclipse. Then, recollecting the Blessed One, the young deva Candima on that occasion recited this verse:
sn2.9:3.1: Then, referring to the young deva Candima, the Blessed One addressed Rahu, lord of the asuras, in verse:
sn2.9:5.1: Then Rahu, lord of the asuras, released the young deva Candima and hurriedly approached Vepacitti, lord of the asuras.[n.157] Although both Rahu and Vepacitti are described as "lords of the asuras" (asurinda), it seems that Vepacitti is the overlord and Rahu a subordinate. Vepacitti is the perennial antagonist of Sakka, lord of the devas, as seen at SN11.4, 11:5, SN11.23, and SN35.248. Having approached, shocked and terrified, he stood to one side. Then, as he stood there, Vepacitti, lord of the asuras, addressed him in verse:
